display_title: 1990-03 — More than 300 have already entered PNWOF90 - have you?
body_html:
Page 1

BEARING 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
May-June 1990

More than 300 have already entered PNWOF90 - have you?

[Image: Logo of a tree and house with the number 90]

As of April 13, we have over 300 entries for the 1990 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival from a dozen countries, which already makes this the biggest meet ever held in Washington. And that number doesn't include many North Americans, who still have a few weeks to enter.

Enclosed in this issue of your newsletter is an entry form for PNWOF90, which will be the final meet of the Asia-Pacific O' Championships 7-day Event (see the schedule below for a list of all the APOC events).

The first day of PNWOF will also be a World Cup event and both days will take place in Cle Elum August 17-19. The low rate deadline for PNWOF90 only is July 1. If you want to enter more of the APOC 7-day events, the low rate deadline is June 1, and you will need an APOC entry form, which you can get by sending a self-addressed, stamped 8½ x 11 envelope to:

Harvey Friedman
c/o Cascade Orienteering Club
P.O. Box 31375
Seattle, WA 98103

Don't miss the excitement of meeting orienteers from around the world. Enter today!

[Image: Logo for World Cup Orienteering 1990]

Club meeting May 17

Our next club meeting will be on Thursday, May 17 at 7:00 p.m. at the University Branch Library (50th and Roosevelt) in Seattle. The featured presentation will be a "See How They Ran" segment about the May 13-14 U.S. Long-O & Relay Championships in San Diego. Everyone is welcome, so come and enjoy seeing how others get lost.

Newsletter editor wanted

After nearly four years, I have decided to retire and let someone else experience the joys and frustrations of editing the club newsletter. Although I have enjoyed (and sometimes hated) doing the newsletter, I can no longer give the job the time it deserves. So, if anyone out there wants to take over, beginning immediately, please give me a call at 488-7471, and let me know.
Debbie Newell, editor

Local orienteers named to U.S. Team

Four orienteers from the Puget Sound area were chosen as members of the U.S. Orienteering Team for 1990. Nathan Tharp, of the Nisqually Orienteers, was one of 10 people named to the U.S. Junior Men's "A" Team, while Jeff Coker, also of Nisqually, was one of 6 named to the Junior Men's "B" Team. And yet another Nisqually member was honored, as Rachel Tharp was named as one of 10 on the Junior Women's Development Team.
And, just to show it's not only young Nisquallyites who know where they are, Debbie Newell, of the Cascade Orienteering Club, was selected as one of the 9-member U.S. Women's "B" Team (the "A" Team has only 2 members this year).
Congratulations, and keep up the good work.

To Russia, With O'

Several local orienteers, including Mike Schuh, will be traveling to the Soviet Union to participate in a multi-day orienteering meet. The trip is part of an exchange program between Olympia and its sister city in the U.S.S.R.

PIG WAR CHALLENGE II

Don't miss these two island events! The two-meet Pig War Challenge was initiated to commemorate the near war between Britain and the U.S. caused by an indiscriminate British pig in the San Juan Islands. At the first Pig War Challenge, held last year, the Canadians walked away with the somewhat ostentatious, but highly sought-after perpetual trophy. The first half of this year's Challenge will take place on Newcastle Island May 26-27, while the second half will be the Washington State Championships held at Moran State Park on Orcas Island June 9-10.

Newcastle Island
Newcastle Island is across a short strait from Nanaimo, Vancouver Island. Ferries to Nanaimo run from both Horseshoe Bay in North Vancouver, and, beginning this year, from Tsawwassen in South Vancouver. From Nanaimo, you may walk the 1.5 miles to the passenger-only Newcastle Island Ferry, or take your car. Camping is available on Newcastle, (restrooms, but no showers) and the fee is included in your meet registration. The island also has a snack bar, and a ferry runs to neighboring Protection Island and the Dingy Dock Pub, a popular aprés O spot. Pre-registration is required for the meet, and must be postmarked by May 14. For an entry form, please contact Ardis Dull at 364-4062. Or call the meet director, Kris Battistuzzi, at (604) 743-9678.

Orcas Island - Washington State Championships
The American half of the Pig War will be on beautiful Orcas Island June 9-10. Preregistration is not required. Registration and starts are 11 a.m.- 1 p.m. Saturday and 9-11 a.m. Sunday. Fees are $6 for one day or $11 for both days (under 18 $5 one day, $9 both). Camping is available at the park, and campsites may be reserved (highly recommended) if done in writing at least 2 weeks in advance. Call the State Parks Information Center at 1-800-562-0990 or Moran State Park at 376-2326 for more information. If you can help at the meet, please call Ruth McCormick For more information, call Ardis Dull at 364-4062.
Please try to carpool. Getting on a ferry either coming or going is very difficult on June weekends, and parking at the park is also very tight. Come to the May 17th Club Meeting for fliers, camping forms, ferry schedules, and carpooling information.

Page 5

the Washington State Orienteering Association presents
1990 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival
August 17-19

Cle Elum, Washington

This application form is for those orienteers who will enter PNWOF only. If you wish to enter any of the Canadian orienteering days of the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Championships 90 event or be eligible for an APOC90 Seven Day Award, you must use the regular APOC90 application form. These forms are available from Harvey Friedman at any Cascade Orienteering Club meet.

Join in the world class atmosphere at the Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival. This year, Day 1 of PNWOF will be held in conjunction with a World Cup event. Prequalified World Cup teams will compete on the same map that the PNWOF courses will be run on; hence, we need to be vague about the exact location of the competition area. PNWOF will be held in Kittitas County, Washington about 150 km (90 mi) east of Seattle or about 320km (200 mi) west of Spokane by I-90. Participants arrange their own accommodations.

Meet Director - Harvey Friedman (206)784-2774. Registrars - Robert Miller and Sandy Kish Miller (206)784-5939 days. Course Designers - Bob & Mary Forbes, Fred Veler, and Anne York.

Maps - 1:15,000, IOF 5 color, 5-meter contour interval. Day 1: new map; base map by ProOrienteering; fieldchecked 1989 by Åre Kristiansen & Vidar Strand. Day 2: 1990 re-fieldchecked and redrawn version of 1981 Cle Elum Ridge map.

Terrain - the forest is predominantly ponderosa pine of variable runnability. Day 1: abandoned coal mining operations add an interesting complexity to the moderately steep terrain which ranges in elevation from 500 to 1090 meters.

Competition Center - Thursday - the lobby of Courson Hall at Central Washington University, 8th St. & Walnut in Ellensburg; Friday-Sunday - Cle Elum High School, Rte. 4, Cle Elum. Check the schedule below for the hours open.

Competitive Classes - please see the attached entry form for classes offered. Day-of-meet registration is available only for wayfarer's courses (see below). You must preregister for competitive classes.

Awards for the top three finishers in each competitive class. Classes may be combined if there are insufficient entries.

Wayfarer Courses - each day there will be three wayfarer courses: beginner, intermediate and advanced. For wayfarer courses, registration is on the day of the event; there is no pregistration. Instruction and limited compass rental will be available. The registration fee for wayfarer courses will be $5.00 per map, per day.

A String-O will be available each day. The registration fee for the string-O will be $1.00 per map, per day.

A Model Event will be offered on Friday, August 17 for anyone, cost $4.00. A model event is a non-competitive event designed to simulate the conditions that will be present during the actual meet. The map for the model event was produced by the same people who produced the Day 1 competition map. You may do any of the available courses, but no times will be recorded.

Child Care is available (only while competing, please) at $1 per child, per hour. Please indicate on the registration form if needed.

Whistles are required of all entrants, on all courses and will be checked at Start Areas. They will be on sale at the event center.

T-shirts (100% cotton w/short sleeves) celebrating both the World Cup and PNWOF can be ordered on the registration form.

Saturday Night Social - there will be a Social Event at the Central Washington University Student Union Ballroom on Saturday, August 18 at 8 p.m. Although details are still to be worked out, we may have a dance. The expected cost, payable at the door, will be approximately $5.00 per person, with a discount for those staying at CWU. Come join the fun!

Cascade Chatter

compiled by Harvey Friedman

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 25 April. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Rich Anter, Boy Scout Troop 325, Patricia Castaldi, Steve & Judy Hill family, Amanda Kolehmainen, Steve Layman & Laura Morin family, Ron & Julie Lee, Roger Leed, Michael McCarthy, Brad Miner, John Nitardy, Smitty Parratt, Barry Reiss, Roger Sharp, Mark E. Sinclair, Tom Travis, Walt & Nancy Turley, Jan Vrana family, Dan Waugh family, Ken Wiljanen, and Bob Williamson.

If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any special events. If you want to get your membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me, at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late or -- lost.

Now on to the fun stuff...Former club member Ingemar Jansson and Helga Haverstad are the proud parents of a second son Eirik. They have moved into their new house in Tranby, which is in Norway between Oslo and Drammen, and are enjoying the added space. Ingemar left his job with DEC and is now working for Oracle...Cascade's own Debbie Newell surprised most of the east coast orienteering cognoscenti by finishing a strong second to Peggy Dickison in the women's open class at the Intercollegiate championship meet back in Rochester 14-15 April. That fine showing assures her a place representing the U.S. on the World Cup team. Congratulatons, Debbie...Tony Paulson and Joan Benderson are proud parents of their second child Monica, born last February. Their son Philip is adjusting to the new addition. Maybe the whole family will get involved with O' again with APOC90 and PNWOF coming in August...This next item is not about an individual or family but rather an event. Three countries have indicated that they intend to enter a total of 15 men and 12 women in the World Cup that we are hosting next August. As you all realize by now this World Cup event will be part of the bigger event PNWOF90. The Pacific Northwest O' Festival 17-19 August will be the biggest and most enjoyable meet on this continent until the 1993 World Championship back east. As of 13 April, we already have 314 people from a dozen countries signed up for both days; we expect a big surge 1 June and again 1 July. All you orienteers who only come to 1 or 2 meets a year, now is your chance. Be a part of the meet either by volunteering to help out (you will still be able to do a course) or just sign up and run. Competitive class just means that you do the course by yourself, not that everyone is supercompetitive. The courses will be similar to what you are used to and the people from other parts of the world want to meet new friends same as you would, so participate. In any event, do your part by talking to the foreign visitors and help convince everyone that we are hospitable in the northwest. If you can't participate yourself, perhaps some of your equipment can. We need about 100 folding chairs, 20-30 folding tables, water jugs, large tents or tarps, donations of wood, hooks & eyes, and people to produce the roughly 1000 results boards that we will need (depending upon total entries) and a truck or two or more to transport the stuff over to the Cle Elum sites, etc. Equipment coordinator Dave Lilly is compiling a list of what will be needed, but you don't have to wait. Contact him (preferably in writing) or me with what you can lend to help make this a great meet...If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.

block_index.text: BEARING 315 The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club May-June 1990
More than 300 have already entered PNWOF90 - have you?
[Image: Logo of a tree and house with the number 90]

As of April 13, we have over 300 entries for the 1990 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival from a dozen countries, which already makes this the biggest meet ever held in Washington. And that number doesn't include many North Americans, who still have a few weeks to enter.

Enclosed in this issue of your newsletter is an entry form for PNWOF90, which will be the final meet of the Asia-Pacific O' Championships 7-day Event (see the schedule below for a list of all the APOC events).

The first day of PNWOF will also be a World Cup event and both days will take place in Cle Elum August 17-19. The low rate deadline for PNWOF90 only is July 1. If you want to enter more of the APOC 7-day events, the low rate deadline is June 1, and you will need an APOC entry form, which you can get by sending a self-addressed, stamped 8½ x 11 envelope to:
Harvey Friedman c/o Cascade Orienteering Club P.O. Box 31375 Seattle, WA 98103
Don't miss the excitement of meeting orienteers from around the world. Enter today!
North American Orienteering Championships & World Cup event, Innisfail, Alberta
North American Orienteering Championships Innisfail, Alberta
Orienteering Skills Clinic conducted by the Norwegian National Orienteering Team
Pacific Northwest O' Festival & World Cup event Cle Elum, Washington
Pacific Northwest O' Festival Cle Elum, Washington

Our next club meeting will be on Thursday, May 17 at 7:00 p.m. at the University Branch Library (50th and Roosevelt) in Seattle. The featured presentation will be a "See How They Ran" segment about the May 13-14 U.S. Long-O & Relay Championships in San Diego. Everyone is welcome, so come and enjoy seeing how others get lost.

After nearly four years, I have decided to retire and let someone else experience the joys and frustrations of editing the club newsletter. Although I have enjoyed (and sometimes hated) doing the newsletter, I can no longer give the job the time it deserves. So, if anyone out there wants to take over, beginning immediately, please give me a call at 488-7471, and let me know. Debbie Newell, editor

Local orienteers named to U.S. Team

Four orienteers from the Puget Sound area were chosen as members of the U.S. Orienteering Team for 1990. Nathan Tharp, of the Nisqually Orienteers, was one of 10 people named to the U.S. Junior Men's "A" Team, while Jeff Coker, also of Nisqually, was one of 6 named to the Junior Men's "B" Team. And yet another Nisqually member was honored, as Rachel Tharp was named as one of 10 on the Junior Women's Development Team. And, just to show it's not only young Nisquallyites who know where they are, Debbie Newell, of the Cascade Orienteering Club, was selected as one of the 9-member U.S. Women's "B" Team (the "A" Team has only 2 members this year). Congratulations, and keep up the good work.

Several local orienteers, including Mike Schuh, will be traveling to the Soviet Union to participate in a multi-day orienteering meet. The trip is part of an exchange program between Olympia and its sister city in the U.S.S.R.

Don't miss these two island events! The two-meet Pig War Challenge was initiated to commemorate the near war between Britain and the U.S. caused by an indiscriminate British pig in the San Juan Islands. At the first Pig War Challenge, held last year, the Canadians walked away with the somewhat ostentatious, but highly sought-after perpetual trophy. The first half of this year's Challenge will take place on Newcastle Island May 26-27, while the second half will be the Washington State Championships held at Moran State Park on Orcas Island June 9-10.

Newcastle Island Newcastle Island is across a short strait from Nanaimo, Vancouver Island. Ferries to Nanaimo run from both Horseshoe Bay in North Vancouver, and, beginning this year, from Tsawwassen in South Vancouver. From Nanaimo, you may walk the 1.5 miles to the passenger-only Newcastle Island Ferry, or take your car. Camping is available on Newcastle, (restrooms, but no showers) and the fee is included in your meet registration. The island also has a snack bar, and a ferry runs to neighboring Protection Island and the Dingy Dock Pub, a popular aprés O spot. Pre-registration is required for the meet, and must be postmarked by May 14. For an entry form, please contact Ardis Dull at 364-4062. Or call the meet director, Kris Battistuzzi, at (604) 743-9678.

Orcas Island - Washington State Championships The American half of the Pig War will be on beautiful Orcas Island June 9-10. Preregistration is not required. Registration and starts are 11 a.m.- 1 p.m. Saturday and 9-11 a.m. Sunday. Fees are $6 for one day or $11 for both days (under 18 $5 one day, $9 both). Camping is available at the park, and campsites may be reserved (highly recommended) if done in writing at least 2 weeks in advance. Call the State Parks Information Center at 1-800-562-0990 or Moran State Park at 376-2326 for more information. If you can help at the meet, please call Ruth McCormick For more information, call Ardis Dull at 364-4062. Please try to carpool. Getting on a ferry either coming or going is very difficult on June weekends, and parking at the park is also very tight. Come to the May 17th Club Meeting for fliers, camping forms, ferry schedules, and carpooling information.

Club Key COC - Cascade O Club; EOC - Ellensburg O Club; EWOC - Eastern Washington O Club; NISQ - Nisqually Orienteers; SAMM - Sammamish O Club; WSOA - Washington State O Assoc. CVO - Cowichan Valley Orienteers; SOCK - Sage Orienteering Club of Kamloops; VO - VictOrienteers
the Washington State Orienteering Association presents 1990 Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival August 17-19

This application form is for those orienteers who will enter PNWOF only. If you wish to enter any of the Canadian orienteering days of the Asia-Pacific Orienteering Championships 90 event or be eligible for an APOC90 Seven Day Award, you must use the regular APOC90 application form. These forms are available from Harvey Friedman at any Cascade Orienteering Club meet.

Join in the world class atmosphere at the Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival. This year, Day 1 of PNWOF will be held in conjunction with a World Cup event. Prequalified World Cup teams will compete on the same map that the PNWOF courses will be run on; hence, we need to be vague about the exact location of the competition area. PNWOF will be held in Kittitas County, Washington about 150 km (90 mi) east of Seattle or about 320km (200 mi) west of Spokane by I-90. Participants arrange their own accommodations.
Meet Director - Harvey Friedman (206)784-2774. Registrars - Robert Miller and Sandy Kish Miller (206)784-5939 days. Course Designers - Bob & Mary Forbes, Fred Veler, and Anne York.

Maps - 1:15,000, IOF 5 color, 5-meter contour interval. Day 1: new map; base map by ProOrienteering; fieldchecked 1989 by Åre Kristiansen & Vidar Strand. Day 2: 1990 re-fieldchecked and redrawn version of 1981 Cle Elum Ridge map.

Terrain - the forest is predominantly ponderosa pine of variable runnability. Day 1: abandoned coal mining operations add an interesting complexity to the moderately steep terrain which ranges in elevation from 500 to 1090 meters.

Competition Center - Thursday - the lobby of Courson Hall at Central Washington University, 8th St. & Walnut in Ellensburg; Friday-Sunday - Cle Elum High School, Rte. 4, Cle Elum. Check the schedule below for the hours open.

Competitive Classes - please see the attached entry form for classes offered. Day-of-meet registration is available only for wayfarer's courses (see below). You must preregister for competitive classes.
Awards for the top three finishers in each competitive class. Classes may be combined if there are insufficient entries.

Wayfarer Courses - each day there will be three wayfarer courses: beginner, intermediate and advanced. For wayfarer courses, registration is on the day of the event; there is no pregistration. Instruction and limited compass rental will be available. The registration fee for wayfarer courses will be $5.00 per map, per day.
A String-O will be available each day. The registration fee for the string-O will be $1.00 per map, per day.

A Model Event will be offered on Friday, August 17 for anyone, cost $4.00. A model event is a non-competitive event designed to simulate the conditions that will be present during the actual meet. The map for the model event was produced by the same people who produced the Day 1 competition map. You may do any of the available courses, but no times will be recorded.
Child Care is available (only while competing, please) at $1 per child, per hour. Please indicate on the registration form if needed.
Whistles are required of all entrants, on all courses and will be checked at Start Areas. They will be on sale at the event center.
T-shirts (100% cotton w/short sleeves) celebrating both the World Cup and PNWOF can be ordered on the registration form.

Saturday Night Social - there will be a Social Event at the Central Washington University Student Union Ballroom on Saturday, August 18 at 8 p.m. Although details are still to be worked out, we may have a dance. The expected cost, payable at the door, will be approximately $5.00 per person, with a discount for those staying at CWU. Come join the fun!

If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late or -- lost.

Now on to the fun stuff...Former club member Ingemar Jansson and Helga Haverstad are the proud parents of a second son Eirik. They have moved into their new house in Tranby, which is in Norway between Oslo and Drammen, and are enjoying the added space. Ingemar left his job with DEC and is now working for Oracle...Cascade's own Debbie Newell surprised most of the east coast orienteering cognoscenti by finishing a strong second to Peggy Dickison in the women's open class at the Intercollegiate championship meet back in Rochester 14-15 April. That fine showing assures her a place representing the U.S. on the World Cup team. Congratulatons, Debbie...Tony Paulson and Joan Benderson are proud parents of their second child Monica, born last February. Their son Philip is adjusting to the new addition. Maybe the whole family will get involved with O' again with APOC90 and PNWOF coming in August...This next item is not about an individual or family but rather an event. Three countries have indicated that they intend to enter a total of 15 men and 12 women in the World Cup that we are hosting next August. As you all realize by now this World Cup event will be part of the bigger event PNWOF90. The Pacific Northwest O' Festival 17-19 August will be the biggest and most enjoyable meet on this continent until the 1993 World Championship back east. As of 13 April, we already have 314 people from a dozen countries signed up for both days; we expect a big surge 1 June and again 1 July. All you orienteers who only come to 1 or 2 meets a year, now is your chance. Be a part of the meet either by volunteering to help out (you will still be able to do a course) or just sign up and run. Competitive class just means that you do the course by yourself, not that everyone is supercompetitive. The courses will be similar to what you are used to and the people from other parts of the world want to meet new friends same as you would, so participate. In any event, do your part by talking to the foreign visitors and help convince everyone that we are hospitable in the northwest. If you can't participate yourself, perhaps some of your equipment can. We need about 100 folding chairs, 20-30 folding tables, water jugs, large tents or tarps, donations of wood, hooks & eyes, and people to produce the roughly 1000 results boards that we will need (depending upon total entries) and a truck or two or more to transport the stuff over to the Cle Elum sites, etc. Equipment coordinator Dave Lilly is compiling a list of what will be needed, but you don't have to wait. Contact him (preferably in writing) or me with what you can lend to help make this a great meet...If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.
Families (regular fee for first 2 family members, $1.00/person thereafter)
Special events, such as the State Championships and "A" meets, have higher fees.
